1: wayyədabbēr šāʾûl ʾel-yônāṯān bənô wəʾel-kol-ʿăḇādāyw ləhāmîṯ ʾeṯ-Dāwîḏ — the verb hāmît (Hiphil of mûṯ, to cause to die) is official and premeditated, not a heat-of-the-moment impulse. The command goes to Jonathan and all the servants simultaneously — a public execution order. The narrative irony is immediate: Jonathan, the designated heir Saul is trying to protect, will become David’s defender.
2: wayyaggēḏ Yônāṯān ləDāwîḏ — “Jonathan told/reported to David.” The verb nāgaḏ (H5046, to declare openly) is the standard word for formal disclosure. Jonathan’s act is not disobedience to his father but an act of covenantal loyalty to David that takes precedence — a conflict of obligations that the narrator presents without condemning Jonathan.
3: Jonathan’s plan: waʾănî ʾēṣēʾ wəʿāmaḏtî ʾeṣel-ʾāḇî — “I will go out and stand beside my father.” He positions himself as a visible mediator, physically between Saul and the field where David hides. The phrase wəʾăḏabberāh bəḵā — “I will speak for you” — frames Jonathan as David’s legal advocate (mēlîṣ-like function) before the king.
4: Jonathan’s intercession opens with ʾal-yeḥṭāʾ hammelek bəʿaḇdô ḇəDāwîḏ — “let not the king sin against his servant David.” The verb ḥāṭāʾ (to miss the mark/sin) reframes what Saul contemplates as a theological offense, not merely a political or personal action. ʿaḇdô (his servant) appeals to the covenant relationship between king and loyal retainer.
5: wayyāśem ʾeṯ-napšô bəḵappô — “he put his life in his hand” — the idiom for risking death (nāpāš as the seat of life, kap as the palm/cupped hand). Jonathan’s advocacy rests on the factual record: David risked his life, YHWH won a great victory (yəšûʿāh gədôlāh), and Saul himself witnessed it. The appeal to eyewitness testimony structures the argument legally.
6: wayyišmaʿ šāʾûl bəqôl Yônāṯān — “Saul listened to Jonathan’s voice.” šāmaʿ bəqôl (hear + in the voice of someone) is the biblical idiom for obedient heeding — more than hearing, it means yielding. Saul swears an oath by YHWH: ḥay-YHWH ʾim-yûmāṯ — “as YHWH lives, he shall not die.” The oath formula invokes YHWH as guarantor; its subsequent violation by Saul deepens the portrait of a man who treats YHWH’s name as an instrument.
7: Jonathan brings David back to Saul, and David resumes court service. The reconciliation is genuine but temporary — narratively it sets up the relapse of 1 Samuel 19:9-10. The wayyəhî šālôm (and there was peace) of the restored situation is fragile; the Hebrew use of the perfect tense signals a completed state that will be undone.
8: War resumes; David’s military success is described with wayyak bāhem makkāh gədôlāh (he struck them with a great blow). The formulaic victory phrase (cf. 1 Samuel 14:31) frames David’s battlefield role as ongoing — he remains the indispensable warrior even as Saul turns murderous.
9: rûaḥ YHWH rāʿāh ʾel-šāʾûl — “an evil spirit from YHWH came to Saul.” The construction is the same as 1 Samuel 16:14-16: rûaḥ rāʿāh mēʾēt YHWH. Hebrew theology does not assign ultimate causation to any power independent of YHWH; the “evil” spirit is divine in origin. Whether this means YHWH sent it directly or permitted it is a genuine theological debate — but the grammar attributes it to YHWH without hesitation. The result is Saul’s violent episode with the spear.
10: wayyəḇaqēš šāʾûl lənakôṯ bəḥaníṯ — “Saul sought to pin David with the spear.” The verb nāḵāh (Hiphil, to strike/pin) with a spear (ḥănîṯ) occurs three times in this narrative cluster (1 Samuel 18:11; 1 Samuel 19:10; 1 Samuel 20:33); each repetition deepens the portrait of Saul’s escalating violence. wayyippaṭēr mippenê šāʾûl — “he escaped from before Saul” — pāṭar (H6452) means to slip away/escape; a different verb from the usual nûs/brāḥ, suggesting a close, narrow escape.
11: Saul sends agents (malʾāḵîm) to watch David’s house through the night. The intelligence apparatus of Saul’s court is now deployed against YHWH’s anointed. ləšomrô wəhămîṯô babbōqer — “to watch him and kill him in the morning.” Michal’s warning is the domestic counterpart to Jonathan’s public advocacy — the entire household conspires to protect David.
12: wattōreḏ ʾeṯ-Dāwîḏ bəʿaḏ haḥallôn — “she let David down through the window.” The window-escape is a recurring biblical motif for protected flight: Rahab’s spies (Joshua 2:15), Paul in Damascus (2 Corinthians 11:33). wayyēleḵ wayyānos wayyimmālēṭ — three rapid verbs (he went, he fled, he escaped) marking the urgency and success of the escape.
13: tərāpîm (H8655, household idols/teraphim) — Michal places the idol in David’s bed with a cushion of goat hair at its head to mimic a sleeping figure. The tərāpîm were household divine images, associated with family religion and divination (cf. Genesis 31:19-35; Ezekiel 21:21). Their presence in David’s house is notable and unremarked; the narrative assumes their existence without condemnation here, though later reforms will prohibit them (1 Samuel 15:23; 2 Kings 23:24).
14: Michal’s lie: ḥôleh hûʾ — “he is sick.” The deception is a protective lie. The verb ḥālāh (H2470, to be sick/weak) uses the same root as the adjective; the simple statement buys time. The messengers accept it and report back without entering.
15: Saul escalates: bring him to me in his bed — bammittāh — so I can kill him. The persistence of the murderous intent even after Jonathan’s successful intercession (v6) confirms that Saul’s oath was temporary capitulation, not genuine repentance.
16: The deception is discovered: messengers enter and find the tərāpîm and goat hair. The elaborate domestic deception — household idol as body double — has a darkly comic quality; it also demonstrates Michal’s resourcefulness and her readiness to lie to her father to protect her husband.
17: Saul’s confrontation with Michal: lāmmāh kākāh rimmîṯinî — “why have you deceived me thus?” rāmāh (H7411, to deceive/betray) is a strong word — the same root used of Laban’s deception of Jacob (Genesis 29:25). Michal’s excuse — “he said he would kill me if I didn’t let him go” — is likely a further lie; the narrator records it without endorsing it.
18: David’s flight to Samuel at Ramah: wayyaggēḏ lô ʾēṯ kol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh lô šāʾûl — “he told him everything Saul had done to him.” The nāgaḏ (declare) vocabulary marks David’s report as an official complaint to his spiritual authority. Naioth (nayyôṯ, perhaps “dwellings/pastures”) in Ramah was the site of Samuel’s prophetic school — David seeks refuge in the prophetic community.
19: Saul receives intelligence about David’s location. The wayyuggaḏ ləšāʾûl (it was reported to Saul) uses the same nāgaḏ root — information networks work in both directions. The surveillance state Saul has built turns all social relationships into potential intelligence sources.
20: Saul’s first group of agents: wayyarəʾ ʾeṯ-lahăqaṯ hannəḇîʾîm nəḇiʾîm — “they saw the company of prophets prophesying.” lahăqāh (H3862, a company/troop) is a technical term for a prophetic guild or school. rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm came upon the agents, and they too began to prophesy. The irresistible divine Spirit overrides the human mission — YHWH’s protection of David operates by co-opting his pursuers.
21: Second and third groups of agents are sent; both are similarly overtaken. The repetition (three groups) emphasizes the completeness of the protection and intensifies the irony: Saul’s military resources are turned into a prophetic choir. The escalation also mirrors the plagues of Egypt — repeated divine resistance to a persistent human will.
22: Saul himself goes. wayyišʾal wayyōmer — he asks where Samuel and David are; he is told at Naioth in Ramah. The bôr haggādôl (great cistern) at Sechu marks the journey’s midpoint — Saul is still determined but spatially isolated before the Spirit meets him.
23: wayyəhî rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm ʿālāyw gam-hûʾ wayyēleḵ hālôḵ wayyiṯnaббēʾ — “the Spirit of God came upon him too, and he walked along prophesying.” The phrase hālôḵ wayyiṯnaббēʾ — “walking and prophesying” — suggests a continuous ecstatic state during the entire journey, not just upon arrival. The Spirit overrides Saul’s murderous intent before he reaches David.
24: wayyipšaṭ gam-hûʾ bəgādāyw wayyiṯnaббēʾ gam-hûʾ lipnê Šəmûʾēl — “he too stripped off his garments and prophesied before Samuel.” The king lies naked (ʿārôm) all day and all night — total loss of royal dignity. ʿal-kēn yōmərû hăgam šāʾûl bannəḇîʾîm — “therefore they say, ‘Is Saul also among the prophets?’” The proverb (first heard in 1 Samuel 10:11-12) is given its second occurrence; in context it becomes ironic commentary on a king whose divine anointing has been redirected to protect his prey.
